name: Geometry
description: Plane and solid geometry — points, lines, angles, triangles, congruence, similarity, the Pythagorean theorem, circles, area, volume, coordinate geometry, and an introduction to formal proof.

modules.title: Points, Lines & Planes
modules.objectives: Identify and describe points, lines, planes, segments, and rays; apply postulates about points, lines, and planes; measure and add segments and angles.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.1: Geometry Basics (points, lines, planes, segments, rays)
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Basic Geometry Language and Notation
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Basic Geometry Practice (15 problems)
modules.assignment: Draw and label 5 different geometric figures using proper notation. For each figure, list all the points, lines, rays, and segments. Identify which undefined terms (point, line, plane) apply and state two postulates that apply to each figure. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Points, Lines & Planes module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Angle Relationships
modules.objectives: Classify angles by measure; identify complementary, supplementary, vertical, and linear pair angles; use the angle bisector theorem; solve problems using angle relationships.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.1: Angles and Their Measures
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Complementary and Supplementary Angles
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Complementary and Supplementary Angles Practice
modules.assignment: Find all missing angle measures in 5 diagrams involving vertical angles, linear pairs, and angle bisectors. Write an equation for each and solve. Classify each angle as acute, right, obtuse, or straight. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Angle Relationships module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Parallel Lines & Transversals
modules.objectives: Identify corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior, and co-interior angle pairs; use angle relationships to find missing measures; prove lines are parallel. Students will show step-by-step reasoning, check solutions for accuracy, and apply the method to the module assignment or a real problem.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.1: Parallel Lines and Transversals
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Parallel Lines and Transversals
modules.video2Note: Khan Academy — Angles Formed by Parallel Lines and Transversals
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Parallel Lines and Transversals Practice
modules.assignment: Given 3 diagrams with parallel lines cut by transversals, identify all 8 angles, classify each angle pair, and find missing measures. Write a two-step proof showing that two given lines are parallel. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Parallel Lines & Transversals module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Triangles: Classification & Properties
modules.objectives: Classify triangles by sides (scalene, isosceles, equilateral) and angles (acute, right, obtuse); apply the Triangle Sum Theorem; use the Exterior Angle Theorem.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.1: Triangles and Their Properties
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Triangle Angle Sum Theorem
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Angles of a Triangle Practice
modules.assignment: Classify 8 triangles by both sides and angles. For each, find all missing angle measures using the Triangle Sum Theorem. Apply the Exterior Angle Theorem to 5 problems. Submit your work as a document, image, or link showing the steps, evidence, and final response. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Triangles: Classification & Properties module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Congruent Triangles
modules.objectives: Identify congruent triangles using SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, and HL congruence postulates/theorems; write two-column proofs to prove triangle congruence.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Triangle Congruence Postulates (SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, HL)
modules.video2Note: Khan Academy — Congruent Triangles and SSS
modules.assignment: For 8 pairs of triangles, state which congruence postulate (SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, or HL) proves they are congruent, or explain why they cannot be proven congruent. Write a complete two-column proof for 2 of the pairs. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Congruent Triangles module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Similar Triangles
modules.objectives: Identify similar triangles using AA, SSS~, and SAS~ similarity criteria; find missing side lengths using proportional sides and scale factors; apply similarity to real-world problems.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.3: Similar Triangles and Proportions
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Similar Triangles and Proportions
modules.assignment: Determine whether 6 pairs of triangles are similar (state the criterion). For 4 pairs of similar triangles, find all missing side lengths. Set up and solve a real-world shadow or mirror problem using triangle similarity. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Similar Triangles module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: The Pythagorean Theorem
modules.objectives: State and apply the Pythagorean Theorem; use the converse to classify triangles; solve problems involving 45-45-90 and 30-60-90 special right triangles.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.3: The Pythagorean Theorem
modules.assignment: Find the missing side in 8 right triangles using the Pythagorean Theorem. Use the converse to classify 3 triangles as acute, right, or obtuse. Solve 4 problems using 45-45-90 and 30-60-90 triangle relationships. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the The Pythagorean Theorem module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Quadrilaterals & Polygons
modules.objectives: Classify and describe properties of parallelograms, rectangles, rhombuses, squares, and trapezoids; apply the polygon interior angle sum formula; find missing angles and side lengths.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.2: Quadrilaterals and Polygons
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Quadrilateral Properties and Theorems
modules.assignment: Complete a properties chart for parallelograms, rectangles, rhombuses, squares, and trapezoids. Find missing angle and side measures in 6 quadrilateral problems. Calculate the interior angle sum for polygons with 5, 6, 8, and 10 sides. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Quadrilaterals & Polygons module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Circles, Arcs & Angles
modules.objectives: Identify and define parts of a circle (radius, diameter, chord, arc, central angle, inscribed angle); calculate circumference and arc length; apply the inscribed angle theorem.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.2: Circle Properties and Circumference
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Central Angles and Arcs
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Central and Inscribed Angles Practice
modules.assignment: Label a circle diagram with all parts. Find the circumference for 4 circles. Calculate arc length for 5 central angles. Apply the inscribed angle theorem to find 4 missing angle measures. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Circles module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Area, Composite Figures & Applications
modules.objectives: Calculate area of triangles, parallelograms, rectangles, trapezoids, regular polygons, and circles; find area of composite figures by addition and subtraction. Students will show step-by-step reasoning, check solutions for accuracy, and apply the method to the module assignment or a real problem.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.2: Area of Triangles, Quadrilaterals, and Circles
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Area of Circles and Other Polygons
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Area of Triangles and Quadrilaterals Practice
modules.assignment: Calculate area for 2 triangles, 2 parallelograms, 2 trapezoids, and 2 circles. Find the area of 3 composite figures. Solve a real-world problem involving the area of a shaded region. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Area module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Surface Area & Volume
modules.objectives: Calculate surface area and volume of prisms, pyramids, cylinders, cones, and spheres; apply formulas to solve real-world problems.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.4: Volume and Surface Area
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Volume of Prisms, Cones, Cylinders, and Spheres
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Volume of Cones, Cylinders, and Spheres Practice
modules.assignment: Find the surface area and volume of 1 prism, 1 pyramid, 1 cylinder, 1 cone, and 1 sphere. Solve a real-world packaging problem determining which container holds more volume. Submit diagrams with dimensions, formulas, substitutions, units, and final answers; then write a short recommendation explaining which container is better for the situation and what tradeoff surface area creates. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Surface Area & Volume module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Coordinate Geometry
modules.objectives: Apply the distance formula and midpoint formula; find slope and equations of lines in the coordinate plane; graph geometric figures and verify properties using coordinates. Students will show step-by-step reasoning, check solutions for accuracy, and apply the method to the module assignment or a real problem.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9: Coordinate Geometry (distance, midpoint, slope)
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Distance Formula and Midpoint Formula
modules.video2Note: Khan Academy — Distance Formula in Analytic Geometry
modules.assignment: Find the distance and midpoint between 6 pairs of points. Classify a quadrilateral as a rectangle by computing all side lengths and diagonal lengths using the distance formula. Write the equation of 3 lines given geometric information. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Coordinate Geometry module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Transformations
modules.objectives: Perform and describe translations, reflections, rotations, and dilations on the coordinate plane; identify lines of symmetry; compose two transformations.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9: Symmetry and Transformations
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Geometric Transformations
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Transformations Practice (translations, reflections, rotations)
modules.assignment: Plot a triangle on the coordinate plane and apply: (1) a translation, (2) a reflection over the x-axis, (3) a 90° rotation about the origin, (4) a dilation with scale factor 2. For a shape of your choice, identify all lines of symmetry. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Transformations module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Introduction to Logic & Proof
modules.objectives: Write conditional statements; form the converse, inverse, and contrapositive; identify valid logical arguments; construct direct proofs using definitions, postulates, and theorems.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9: Logical Reasoning and Geometric Proof
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Geometric Proofs
modules.assignment: Write the converse, inverse, and contrapositive for 5 conditional statements; determine whether each is true or false. Complete 2 two-column proofs involving angle relationships and parallel lines, supplying the missing statements and reasons. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Introduction to Logic & Proof module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

questions.question: Which of the following is an undefined term in geometry?
questions.explanation: Point, line, and plane are the three undefined terms in Euclidean geometry — they are described but not formally defined.

questions.question: Two angles are supplementary. One angle measures 73°. What is the measure of the other angle?
questions.explanation: Supplementary angles sum to 180°. 180° − 73° = 107°. It connects the correct answer to Geometry midterm concepts and separates it from plausible distractors.

questions.question: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. Angle 1 and Angle 2 are alternate interior angles, and Angle 1 = 65°. What is the measure of Angle 2?
questions.explanation: Alternate interior angles are congruent when lines are parallel, so Angle 2 = 65°.

questions.question: The angles of a triangle are in the ratio 2:3:5. What is the measure of the largest angle?
questions.explanation: The ratio has 10 total parts, so each part is 18°. The largest angle is 5 parts, or 90°.

questions.question: Which congruence postulate is illustrated when two sides and the included angle of one triangle are congruent to two sides and the included angle of another?
questions.explanation: Side-Angle-Side (SAS): two sides and the included (between them) angle determine a unique triangle.

questions.question: Two triangles are similar with a scale factor of 3:5. If a side of the smaller triangle is 12 cm, what is the corresponding side of the larger triangle?
questions.explanation: Scale factor 3:5 means larger/smaller = 5/3. Side = 12 × (5/3) = 20 cm. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Geometry midterm concepts, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

questions.question: A right triangle has legs of 8 cm and 15 cm. What is the length of the hypotenuse?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: 17 cm.

questions.question: In a 45-45-90 triangle, the legs each measure 7 cm. What is the length of the hypotenuse?
questions.explanation: In a 45-45-90 triangle, the hypotenuse equals a leg times √2, so the hypotenuse is 7√2 cm.

questions.question: What is the sum of interior angles of a hexagon?
An inscribed angle in a circle intercepts an arc of 140°. What is the measure of the inscribed angle?
questions.explanation: Inscribed Angle Theorem: inscribed angle = ½ × intercepted arc = ½ × 140° = 70°.

questions.question: What is the contrapositive of the statement: "If it is raining, then the ground is wet"?
options: If it is not raining, then the ground is not wet.
If the ground is wet, then it is raining.
If the ground is not wet, then it is not raining.
If it is raining, then the ground is not wet.
questions.answer: If the ground is not wet, then it is not raining.
questions.explanation: The contrapositive negates and reverses the conditional: 'If not Q, then not P.'

questions.question: A line segment has endpoints A(2, 3) and B(8, 11). What is the length of AB?
questions.explanation: The horizontal change is 6 and the vertical change is 8, so the distance is √(6² + 8²) = 10.

questions.question: In Geometry midterm concepts, Vertical angles are always:
questions.explanation: Vertical angles are the opposite angles formed when two lines intersect; they are always congruent.

questions.question: A 5-meter ladder leans against a wall, with its base 3 meters from the wall. How high up the wall does the ladder reach? Show your work using the Pythagorean Theorem.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: 4 meters.

questions.question: Two angles form a linear pair. One angle is expressed as (3x + 20)° and the other as (x + 40)°. Find the value of x and the measure of each angle.
Which description best defines a ray?
options: A part of a line with one endpoint that extends infinitely in one direction
A line segment with two endpoints and fixed length
A set of all points in a plane equidistant from a center
A flat surface that extends infinitely in all directions
questions.answer: A part of a line with one endpoint that extends infinitely in one direction
questions.explanation: A ray begins at one endpoint and continues without end in one direction. Students need to recognize why A part of a line with one endpoint that extends infinitely in one direction matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

questions.question: Two angles are complementary. One angle is (2x + 10)°. The other is (3x − 5)°. What is the value of x?
questions.explanation: Complementary angles sum to 90°: (2x+10)+(3x−5)=90 → 5x+5=90 → 5x=85 → x=17.

questions.question: Co-interior (same-side interior) angles formed by parallel lines cut by a transversal are:
questions.explanation: Co-interior (consecutive interior) angles are on the same side of the transversal and sum to 180° when lines are parallel.

questions.question: A triangle has an exterior angle of 115°. The two non-adjacent interior angles are (5x)° and (4x + 7)°. What is x?
questions.explanation: Exterior Angle Theorem: exterior angle = sum of remote interior angles. 5x + 4x + 7 = 115 → 9x = 108 → x = 12.

questions.question: Which triangle congruence criterion applies only to right triangles?
questions.explanation: The Hypotenuse-Leg criterion is specifically for proving right triangles congruent.

questions.question: In similar triangles △ABC ~ △DEF, AB = 6, DE = 9, and AC = 8. What is DF?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: 12.

questions.question: In a 30-60-90 triangle, the shorter leg is 5. What is the length of the hypotenuse?
questions.explanation: In a 30-60-90 triangle, hypotenuse = 2 × shorter leg = 2 × 5 = 10. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Geometry final concepts, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

questions.question: A rectangle has opposite sides equal and all four right angles. Which property is unique to a rectangle (but not all parallelograms)?
questions.explanation: In a rectangle, the diagonals are congruent. This is not true of all parallelograms (e.g., a non-rectangular parallelogram has unequal diagonals).

questions.question: A circle has a radius of 6 cm. What is the length of an arc intercepted by a central angle of 120°?
questions.explanation: The arc is 120/360 of the circumference. One third of 2π(6) is 4π cm. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Geometry final concepts, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

questions.question: What is the area of a trapezoid with bases 7 cm and 13 cm and a height of 5 cm?
questions.explanation: A = ½h(b₁ + b₂) = ½ × 5 × (7 + 13) = ½ × 5 × 20 = 50 cm². Students need to recognize why 50 cm² matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

questions.question: A cylinder has radius 4 cm and height 10 cm. What is its volume?
questions.explanation: V = πr²h = π(4²)(10) = π(16)(10) = 160π cm³. This reinforces the Geometry final concepts idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

questions.question: What is the midpoint of the segment with endpoints (−4, 6) and (10, −2)?
A triangle with vertices A(0, 0), B(4, 0), and C(4, 3) is reflected over the y-axis. What are the coordinates of the image of B?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: (−4, 0).

questions.question: Which statement is the converse of: "If a polygon is a square, then it has four right angles"?
options: If a polygon does not have four right angles, then it is not a square.
If a polygon has four right angles, then it is a square.
If a polygon is not a square, then it does not have four right angles.
A polygon is a square if and only if it has four right angles.
questions.answer: If a polygon has four right angles, then it is a square.
questions.explanation: The converse switches the hypothesis and conclusion: 'If Q, then P.' Note that the converse is not necessarily true.

questions.question: A dilation with center at the origin and scale factor 3 maps point P(2, −4) to P'. What are the coordinates of P'?
questions.explanation: Dilation by scale factor k maps (x, y) → (kx, ky). P(2, −4) → (3×2, 3×(−4)) = (6, −12).

questions.question: What is the area of a circle with diameter 14 cm?
questions.explanation: Radius = 7 cm. A = πr² = π(7²) = 49π cm². It connects the correct answer to Geometry final concepts and separates it from plausible distractors.

questions.question: A sphere has a radius of 3 cm. What is its volume?
questions.explanation: The volume of a sphere is (4/3)πr³. With r = 3, the volume is (4/3)π(27) = 36π cm³.

questions.question: A coordinate proof: Points A(0, 0), B(6, 0), C(6, 4), and D(0, 4) form a quadrilateral. Use the distance formula to show that ABCD is a rectangle (all sides meet at right angles, opposite sides equal).
questions.answer: AB = 6, BC = 4, CD = 6, DA = 4; all adjacent sides are perpendicular (one is horizontal, one is vertical), confirming a rectangle.
questions.explanation: AB: √((6−0)²+(0−0)²)=6; BC: √((6−6)²+(4−0)²)=4; CD: √((0−6)²+(4−4)²)=6; DA: √((0−0)²+(0−4)²)=4. Opposite sides equal, adjacent sides perpendicular (slopes 0 and undefined) → rectangle.

questions.question: A conical paper cup has a radius of 4 cm and a height of 9 cm. Find its volume. Then determine how many such cups of water are needed to fill a cylindrical tank with radius 4 cm and height 9 cm.
questions.answer: Cone volume = 48π cm³; Cylinder volume = 144π cm³; 3 cones needed. A strong response should use Geometry final concepts vocabulary, answer the full prompt, and include reasoning or evidence rather than a one-word reply.

questions.question: Triangle ABC has vertices A(1, 2), B(5, 2), and C(3, 6). Find the perimeter of triangle ABC. Round to the nearest tenth if needed.
questions.answer: AB = 4, BC = √20 ≈ 4.5, CA = √20 ≈ 4.5; Perimeter ≈ 13.0 units. A strong response should use Geometry final concepts vocabulary, answer the full prompt, and include reasoning or evidence rather than a one-word reply.

quizQuestions.question: Which undefined geometry term names an exact location represented by a dot?
quizQuestions.explanation: A point has no length, width, or depth — it simply marks a location in space.

quizQuestions.question: A segment is part of a line that:
options: Has one endpoint and extends infinitely
quizQuestions.explanation: A line segment is a portion of a line bounded by two endpoints. It connects the correct answer to Points, Lines & Planes and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: If M is the midpoint of segment AB and AM = 7 cm, what is AB?
quizQuestions.explanation: M is the midpoint, so AM = MB = 7 cm. AB = 7 + 7 = 14 cm. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Points, Lines & Planes.

quizQuestions.question: Which undefined term has no size, shape, or dimension and is represented by a dot?
quizQuestions.explanation: A point identifies an exact location and has no size, shape, or dimension. Students need to recognize why Point matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: What term describes two angles whose measures add to 90°?
quizQuestions.explanation: Complementary angles sum to exactly 90°. Students need to recognize why Complementary matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: A linear pair of angles always sums to:
quizQuestions.explanation: A linear pair consists of two adjacent angles that together form a straight line (180°).

quizQuestions.question: Vertical angles are formed when two lines intersect. They are always:
quizQuestions.explanation: Vertical angles are the non-adjacent angles formed at an intersection; they are always equal in measure.

quizQuestions.question: An angle bisector divides an angle into:
quizQuestions.explanation: By definition, an angle bisector splits an angle into two equal (congruent) halves.

quizQuestions.question: Two angles whose measures sum to 90° are called:
quizQuestions.explanation: Complementary angles have measures that add to 90°. It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in Angle Relationships.

quizQuestions.question: For parallel lines cut by a transversal, what relationship do corresponding angles have?
quizQuestions.explanation: Corresponding angles occupy the same position at each intersection and are congruent when lines are parallel.

quizQuestions.question: Alternate exterior angles are located:
options: On the same side of the transversal, between the parallel lines
On opposite sides of the transversal, between the parallel lines
On opposite sides of the transversal, outside the parallel lines
On the same side, outside the parallel lines
quizQuestions.answer: On opposite sides of the transversal, outside the parallel lines
quizQuestions.explanation: Alternate exterior angles are on opposite sides of the transversal and outside (exterior to) the two parallel lines.

quizQuestions.question: Two lines cut by a transversal have co-interior angles of 75° and 115°. Are the lines parallel?
options: Yes, because co-interior angles are supplementary
No, because co-interior angles must equal 180°
Yes, because co-interior angles are congruent
quizQuestions.explanation: Co-interior angles must sum to 180° for the lines to be parallel. 75° + 115° = 190° ≠ 180°, so the lines are not parallel.

quizQuestions.question: When parallel lines are cut by a transversal, corresponding angles are:
quizQuestions.explanation: Corresponding angles formed by a transversal crossing parallel lines have equal measures.

quizQuestions.question: Where are alternate exterior angles located when two lines are cut by a transversal?
options: On opposite sides of the transversal, outside the two lines
On the same side of the transversal, between the two lines
On opposite sides of the transversal, between the two lines
quizQuestions.answer: On opposite sides of the transversal, outside the two lines
quizQuestions.explanation: Alternate exterior angles lie outside the two lines and on opposite sides of the transversal.

quizQuestions.question: The Triangle Sum Theorem states that the interior angles of a triangle sum to:
quizQuestions.explanation: The three interior angles of any triangle always add up to exactly 180°. It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in Triangles: Classification & Properties.

quizQuestions.question: A triangle with all three sides equal is called:
quizQuestions.explanation: An equilateral triangle has all three sides congruent and all three angles equal to 60°.

quizQuestions.question: In triangle ABC, ∠A = 45° and ∠B = 75°. What is ∠C?
quizQuestions.explanation: ∠C = 180° − 45° − 75° = 60°. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Triangles: Classification & Properties.

quizQuestions.question: An exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two:
options: Interior angles on the same side
quizQuestions.explanation: The Exterior Angle Theorem: an exterior angle equals the sum of the two non-adjacent (remote) interior angles.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Complete this statement: A triangle with one angle greater than 90° is classified as...
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "Obtuse" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: The SSS congruence postulate requires:
options: Two sides and the included angle to be congruent
All three sides to be congruent
Two angles and a side to be congruent
The hypotenuse and one leg to be congruent
quizQuestions.answer: All three sides to be congruent
quizQuestions.explanation: Side-Side-Side (SSS): if all three sides of one triangle are congruent to the corresponding sides of another, the triangles are congruent.

quizQuestions.question: In a two-column proof, what goes in the right column?
quizQuestions.explanation: The left column contains statements and the right column contains the justifications (reasons) for each statement.

quizQuestions.question: In Congruent Triangles, AAS congruence uses:
options: Two angles and the included side
Two angles and a non-included side
quizQuestions.answer: Two angles and a non-included side
quizQuestions.explanation: Angle-Angle-Side (AAS): two angles and a non-included (not between them) side prove congruence.

quizQuestions.question: The HL congruence theorem applies only to:
quizQuestions.explanation: Hypotenuse-Leg (HL) applies only to right triangles, using the hypotenuse and one leg.

quizQuestions.question: If △ABC ≅ △DEF, which pair of angles must be congruent?
quizQuestions.explanation: Corresponding parts of congruent triangles are congruent (CPCTC). A corresponds to D in △ABC ≅ △DEF.

quizQuestions.question: What must be true to use the SSS congruence postulate?
options: All three pairs of corresponding sides are congruent
Two sides and the included angle are congruent
Two angles and the included side are congruent
Only the hypotenuse and one leg are congruent
quizQuestions.answer: All three pairs of corresponding sides are congruent
quizQuestions.explanation: SSS proves triangle congruence when all three pairs of corresponding sides match.

quizQuestions.question: The AA similarity criterion states two triangles are similar if:
options: Two angles of one equal two angles of the other
Two sides and the included angle are proportional
All three angles are equal in both triangles
quizQuestions.answer: Two angles of one equal two angles of the other
quizQuestions.explanation: Angle-Angle (AA): if two pairs of corresponding angles are congruent, the triangles are similar.

quizQuestions.question: In Similar Triangles, Similar triangles have:
quizQuestions.explanation: Similar triangles have the same shape — corresponding angles are congruent and corresponding sides are proportional.

quizQuestions.question: The scale factor of △ABC to △DEF is 2:3. If AB = 8, what is DE?
quizQuestions.explanation: Scale factor AB:DE = 2:3. DE = AB × (3/2) = 8 × 1.5 = 12. It connects the correct answer to Similar Triangles and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: The AA similarity criterion states that two triangles are similar if:
options: Two angles of one triangle are congruent to two angles of the other triangle
All three sides of the two triangles are equal in length
The triangles have the same perimeter
One side and one angle are congruent
quizQuestions.answer: Two angles of one triangle are congruent to two angles of the other triangle
quizQuestions.explanation: If two pairs of angles match, the third pair must also match, so the triangles are similar.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Complete this statement: If two triangles are similar with a scale factor of 1:4, how do their areas compare...
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "1:16" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Complete this statement: A tree casts a 15 m shadow. nearby, a 2 m post casts a 3 m shadow. how tall is the tree...
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "10 m" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: Using the Pythagorean Theorem, what is the hypotenuse when the legs are 6 and 8?
quizQuestions.explanation: c² = 6² + 8² = 36 + 64 = 100. c = 10. It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in The Pythagorean Theorem.

quizQuestions.question: The converse of the Pythagorean Theorem states that if a² + b² = c², then the triangle is:
quizQuestions.explanation: If the square of the longest side equals the sum of squares of the other two sides, the triangle is a right triangle.

quizQuestions.question: A triangle has sides of length 5, 12, and 13. Is it a right triangle?
options: Yes, because all sides are integers
quizQuestions.explanation: 25 + 144 = 169 = 13². The converse of the Pythagorean Theorem confirms this is a right triangle.

quizQuestions.question: In a right triangle with legs a = 6 and b = 8, what is the hypotenuse?
quizQuestions.explanation: By the Pythagorean Theorem, c = √(6² + 8²) = √100 = 10. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in The Pythagorean Theorem, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Complete this statement: In a 30-60-90 triangle, the longer leg is 6√3. what is the shorter leg...
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "6" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: The sum of interior angles of a quadrilateral is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Interior angle sum = (4 − 2) × 180° = 2 × 180° = 360°. Students need to recognize why 360° matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: A parallelogram has one angle of 70°. What are the measures of the remaining three angles?
quizQuestions.explanation: Consecutive angles in a parallelogram are supplementary. The four angles are 70°, 110°, 70°, 110°.

quizQuestions.question: Which quadrilateral has exactly one pair of parallel sides?
quizQuestions.explanation: A trapezoid has exactly one pair of parallel sides (the bases). Parallelograms have two pairs.

quizQuestions.question: The formula for the sum of interior angles of an n-gon is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Any polygon can be divided into (n − 2) triangles, each contributing 180°. This reinforces the Quadrilaterals & Polygons idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: What is the sum of the interior angles of a quadrilateral?
quizQuestions.explanation: A quadrilateral can be divided into two triangles, so its interior angles sum to 2 × 180° = 360°.

quizQuestions.question: The distance from the center of a circle to any point on the circle is called the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The radius is the fixed distance from the center to any point on the circle.

quizQuestions.question: A central angle of 90° in a circle with radius 5 cm subtends an arc. What fraction of the circle's circumference is this arc?
quizQuestions.explanation: 90° is 90/360 = 1/4 of the full circle, so the arc is 1/4 of the circumference.

quizQuestions.question: An inscribed angle that intercepts a semicircle (arc of 180°) measures:
quizQuestions.explanation: Inscribed angle = ½ × intercepted arc = ½ × 180° = 90°. This is Thales' Theorem.

quizQuestions.question: What is the distance from the center of a circle to any point on the circle called?
quizQuestions.explanation: A radius is a segment from the center of a circle to a point on the circle.

quizQuestions.question: A central angle of 90° subtends an arc of what fraction of the circle's circumference?
quizQuestions.explanation: A 90° central angle is 90/360 of the circle, which simplifies to 1/4. Students need to recognize why 1/4 matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: What is the area of a triangle with base 12 cm and height 7 cm?
quizQuestions.explanation: A = ½bh = ½ × 12 × 7 = 42 cm². The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Area, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

quizQuestions.question: What is the area of a parallelogram with base 9 m and height 5 m?
quizQuestions.explanation: A = bh = 9 × 5 = 45 m². Students need to recognize why 45 m² matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: A square has area 64 cm². What is the length of its side?
quizQuestions.explanation: A = s². s = √64 = 8 cm. Students need to recognize why 8 cm matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: A composite figure consists of a rectangle (5 × 4) with a semicircle (radius 2) on top. What is the area of the composite figure?
quizQuestions.explanation: Rectangle area = 5 × 4 = 20. Semicircle area = ½π(2²) = 2π. Total = 20 + 2π.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Which formula gives the area of a regular polygon with perimeter p and apothem a?
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "A = ½Pa" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Complete this statement: A composite figure consists of a rectangle (5 × 4) with a semicircle (radius 2) on top. what is the area of the composite figure...
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "20 + 2π" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: What is the volume of a rectangular prism with length 4, width 3, and height 5?
quizQuestions.explanation: V = l × w × h = 4 × 3 × 5 = 60 cubic units. This reinforces the Surface Area & Volume idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: The volume of a pyramid is what fraction of the volume of a prism with the same base and height?
quizQuestions.explanation: V_pyramid = ⅓ × Base Area × height. It is exactly one-third of the corresponding prism's volume.

quizQuestions.question: What is the total surface area of a cube with side length 4 cm?
quizQuestions.explanation: A cube has 6 faces, each with area s². SA = 6 × (4)² = 6 × 16 = 96 cm². This reinforces the Surface Area & Volume idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: A cone has radius 3 and height 4. What is its volume?
quizQuestions.explanation: V = ⅓πr²h = ⅓π(9)(4) = 12π. Students need to recognize why 12π matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: What is the volume of a sphere with radius 6 cm?
quizQuestions.explanation: V = (4/3)πr³ = (4/3)π(6)³ = (4/3)π(216) = 288π cm³. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Surface Area & Volume, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

quizQuestions.question: Which option best completes this statement: The volume of a rectangular prism with length 4, width 3, and height 5 is...
quizQuestions.explanation: The best answer is 60. It directly fits the prompt, while the other choices reflect different course ideas, incomplete definitions, or common mistakes.

quizQuestions.question: What is the distance between points (1, 4) and (5, 7)?
quizQuestions.explanation: d = √((5−1)² + (7−4)²) = √(16 + 9) = √25 = 5. It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in Coordinate Geometry.

quizQuestions.question: What is the midpoint of the segment from (2, 8) to (6, 2)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Midpoint = ((2+6)/2, (8+2)/2) = (4, 5). The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Coordinate Geometry, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

quizQuestions.question: What is the slope of the line through (0, 3) and (4, 11)?
quizQuestions.explanation: m = (11−3)/(4−0) = 8/4 = 2. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Coordinate Geometry, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

quizQuestions.question: Which option best completes this statement: The distance between points (1, 4) and (5, 7) is...
quizQuestions.explanation: The best answer is 5. It directly fits the prompt, while the other choices reflect different course ideas, incomplete definitions, or common mistakes.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Define or explain: Two points on a circle are a(0, 0) and b(6, 0). what is the length of chord ab.
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "6" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: Fill in the blank: ___ is the equation of a line with slope 3 that passes through the origin.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: y = 3x.

quizQuestions.question: A translation moves a point (3, −2) by the vector ⟨−4, 5⟩. Where does the point land?
quizQuestions.explanation: (3 + (−4), −2 + 5) = (−1, 3). It connects the correct answer to Transformations and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: Reflecting the point (3, −5) over the x-axis gives:
quizQuestions.explanation: Reflection over x-axis: (x, y) → (x, −y). (3, −5) → (3, 5). This reinforces the Transformations idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: Rotating the point (2, 3) by 90° counterclockwise about the origin gives:
quizQuestions.explanation: 90° CCW rotation rule: (x, y) → (−y, x). (2, 3) → (−3, 2). This reinforces the Transformations idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: A translation moves the point (3, −2) by the vector ⟨−4, 5⟩. Where does the point land?
quizQuestions.explanation: Add the vector components to the point: 3 + (−4) = −1 and −2 + 5 = 3. It connects the correct answer to Transformations and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Which transformation preserves both size and shape (is an isometry)?
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "Rotation" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: The hypothesis of the conditional statement 'If a shape is a square, then it has four equal sides' is:
options: It does not have four equal sides
quizQuestions.explanation: In a conditional 'If P, then Q,' P is the hypothesis (the 'if' part). This reinforces the Introduction to Logic & Proof idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: The inverse of 'If P, then Q' is:
options: If not P, then not Q
If not Q, then not P
quizQuestions.answer: If not P, then not Q
quizQuestions.explanation: The inverse negates both the hypothesis and conclusion of the original conditional.

quizQuestions.question: Which form of a conditional is always logically equivalent to the original?
quizQuestions.explanation: The contrapositive ('If not Q, then not P') is always logically equivalent to the original conditional.

quizQuestions.question: In a two-column proof, the first statement is typically:
options: The conclusion you want to prove
A theorem from a previous chapter
quizQuestions.explanation: A proof begins with what is given (known) and uses logical steps to reach the conclusion.

quizQuestions.question: What is the hypothesis of the conditional statement "If a shape is a square, then it has four equal sides"?
quizQuestions.explanation: The hypothesis is the "if" part of a conditional statement. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Introduction to Logic & Proof, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.
